dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 2 for a reported unknown problem, person down on the side of the roadway.
Hi, I'm EMT Brad. We're with the rescue squad. Can you or someone with you tell me what happened?
He was found unconscious on the street a few minutes ago.
Let me check on him now. We're going to perform a quick exam.
Opens eyes to verbal command, but is unsure of the event.
Verbal responses are present, but disoriented to time and place.
Follows simple commands with movement returning to all extremities.
Airway is patent and self-maintained. Patient breathing slightly irregularly.
Breathing rate is 12 per minute. No signs of accessory muscle use noted.
BP is 172 over 68, pulse is 115. Jugular vein distention is present.
Oxygen saturation is 91%. Oxygen is administered via NRB at 15 LPM now.
Blood pressure is 172 over 68, still elevated on initial check.
Respiratory rate is 12 per minute with no signs of respiratory distress observed.
Mr. Greg, can you tell me your name and the last thing you remember?
My name is Greg... but I don't remember how I got here. Just feels strange.
He was found lying on the ground, unresponsive. I called 911 immediately.
You have history of seizures and TBI-still taking Keppra for that, yes?
Yes, Keppra is all I take at the moment. Didn't take anything else recently.
Any known allergies right now, like to medications or food?
I don't believe so. No known allergies at this point in time.
Any pain or discomfort at the moment, Mr. Greg? Feel like your head is tight or bruised?
No pain at all. Just feel foggy and disoriented like someone else is in my head.
Let me shine a light in your pupils to check for equal response now. That's normal.
Pupils at 1 mm and non-reactive-pinpoint size. No signs of trauma or pallor on the skin.
Skin is warm, diaphoretic, and intact with no visible lacerations or rashes noted.
No bruises or purpura noted anywhere on the skin surface. Atraumatic overall.
Let me listen to your lungs while you breathe in and out deep. Clear bilaterally.
No adventitious sounds in lungs. Rib cage is stable and intact.
Pressing gently now. Any tenderness or rigidity in your belly? Statement will inform EMD.
Abdomen is soft, non-tender, and non-distended as observed during exam.
Feels okay now. Nothing quite stands out as unusable or broken.
How about your arms and legs? Can you move them all of them right now?
No movement initially on all four limbs; movement returns to all four. Positive PMS x4 noted.
Confidence in limbs is coming back. Don't feel paralyzed anymore.
Heart rate remains elevated at 115 now, as part of tachycardia.
Blood sugar at 222-which remains high on initial assessment.
EKG still sinus tach. No new concerning changes to report at this time.
Field impression is opioid overdose. Activating Medical Overdose, Protocol 7-1.
Initiating 15 LPM of O2 and IV setup now. Preparing for Narcan.
Administering Narcan 0.5 mg intravenous to reverse possible opioid effect on CNS.
IV started in the left arm. Delivered via 20-gauge catheter for Narcan administration.
Narcan administered quickly. Now observing for response in alertness and motor return.
SpO2 increased to 98%. Oxygen is being well tolerated.
Blood pressure dropped slightly to 143 over 84. No hypotension signs reported.
Respiratory rate slightly increased to 14 per minute. Good sign of tach over tachypnea.
EKG is still in sinus tach. No acute changes to report for now.
Blood glucose lowered to 0. Potentially due to IV fluid commencement.
Alertness improved significantly now. No report of pain remains constant.
Transportation facility plans?
You decide. I'm not in the right headspace to decide for myself right now.
Arranging transport to Richmond Community Hospital before it gets missed.
Moving him now to the stretcher. Do you need assistance moving into position?
Yes, proceed at your lead.
Secured in semi-fowler's position at 30 degrees for breathing support.
Feels okay. No stress in that position. Breathing relaxed.
Coping well with transportation? Any signs that this changes now?
Everything's stable. Ready when you are.
Transport is cheeky today. 10 minutes to get to Richmond Hospital estimated.
Wallet and cell phone are secured with the patient for hospital access or identification.
Just handed off patient report package to Shelley Johnson in the ER.
Any supplemental findings for hospital to note?
Don't think I had a seizure today. Woke up on the street with this strange mental state.
For clarity, SPO2 increased from 91 to 98%, per EKG, tach sustained. Pinpoint pupils, no report of N/V/D.
Noted JVD at first contact and no neck injuries. Still stable now.
Initial assessment supported no reportable overdose or poisoning at time of PCR.
IV was successful in left arm. Narcan administered as 0.5 mg intravenous route.
All notes and documentation have been completed for emergency room intake.
Noted atraumatic assessment. Pelvis is stable through the whole scene.
Cardiac EKG load is remembered to be 4-lead setup with no anomalies post setup.
EKG shows sinus tach since arrival with no acute change pretransport.
Movement was smooth, patient cooperated. Transported in semi-fowler's per medical rationale.
IV and Narcan administered with standing order guidance. No complaints post.
Mentioned CA+OX3/4 when patient regained alertness. Otherwise GCS at 11 per PCR.
Initial assessment showed no trauma to pelvis, head, chest, or extremities.
All interventions were performed consistent with Protocol 7-1 for overdose/poisoning.
No suspected spinal immobilization needed. Movement went safely. No narcotinic antagonism involved.
Pelvis remains stable now and during full scene time. No complaints of any lower body trauma.
Transport was smooth, and vitals stayed stable through all stages.
No recent history of drug use or alcohol from patient during assessment.
Blood sugar remained high but dropped after IV admin, as previously noted.
Patient was lifted from street with care not to worsen any spinal or head injury.
All findings today are consistent with initial suspicion of opioid ingestion.
Diaphoresis noted at first contact, consistent with initial PCR.
Patient remains stable and is able to move limbs and sense space.
Keppra is the only medication used. RN in ER will be notified about medication.
Narcan was observed to improve mental state and motor coordination within 5 minutes.
No adverse reaction to Narcan or IV documented, but full care at hospital is recommended.
Confirmed: 4-lead ECG monitoring implemented per standing order and supported in EKG report.
No changes noted in GCS beyond assisted level. Follow-up after initial placement remains solid.
Oxygen continues on NRB mask, at patient's request, during full transport window.
Initial PCR glucose reported at 222, went to 0 after IV initiation. All in notes.
Patient will pivot into ER bed upon arrival, able to assist and ambulate short distances.
All equipment and scene notes are being handed over with patient. Thank you for the support.
No patient refusal, no capability issues at this point. All decisions made with hospital.
All interventions documented per standing order using EKG IV & Narcan for possible opioid.
JVD remains resolved with no deterioration observed during scene and transport time.
PMS x4 final notes indicate all limbs returned movement and sensation to baseline at last check.
